title: VCU vs Illinois — Round of 32 Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • Round of 32 • Greenville, SC
venue: Bon Secours Wellness Arena, Greenville, SC • 3:00 PM ET • TBS

stakes.callout: The team that staged the largest first-round comeback in NCAA Tournament history meets the team that set an Illinois tournament scoring record with 105 points. VCU erased a 19-point deficit against North Carolina and won in overtime on Terrence Hill Jr.'s 34-point explosion. Illinois obliterated Penn 105-70 behind Mirkovic's 29 and 17. One team is riding pure adrenaline. The other has the #1 offense in KenPom. This is going to be fun.

cards.label: For VCU
cards.value: Just pulled off the largest first-round comeback in tournament history — down 19 to North Carolina, won 82-78 in overtime. Terrence Hill Jr. scored 34 with a stepback three to seal it. First tournament win since 2016. A Sweet 16 would echo the 2011 Cinderella run that went all the way to the Final Four.

cards.label: For Illinois
cards.value: Scored 105 points — an Illinois NCAA Tournament record. Mirkovic had 29 and 17 (tournament rebound record for Illinois). Wagler had 18/7/7. The #1 offense in KenPom looks every bit of it. A Sweet 16 would continue Underwood's best sustained run in Champaign.

cards.label: The Emotional Edge
cards.value: VCU just played the game of their lives — and overtime. That kind of emotional peak can carry a team to incredible heights or leave them completely drained. Illinois played a stress-free blowout and rested starters early. Fresh legs vs. adrenaline — which wins?

cards.label: Offense vs. Depth
cards.value: Illinois has the #1 offense in KenPom (125.5 adjusted) with five double-figure scorers. VCU counters with the 8th-best bench in America (34 PPG), seven players at 20+ minutes, and wave after wave of fresh bodies. VCU wants to wear Illinois down. Illinois wants to bury them before that happens.

styles.title: Depth, Pressure, and the Hottest Player in March
bullets: First-round magic: Erased a 19-point deficit against UNC — the largest first-round comeback in tournament history. Won 82-78 in overtime. Terrence Hill Jr. scored 34 (7-of-10 from three), including the stepback dagger with 15 seconds left in OT.

Relentless depth: Seven players log 20+ minutes. Bench averages 34 PPG (8th nationally). No starter plays more than 28 minutes. VCU wears teams down with waves of fresh bodies — UNC's six-man rotation crumbled in the second half and OT.

Layups, threes, and free throws: 80.8% of shots come from three-point range or at the rim. They shoot 36.7% from three and attempt 25.4 free throws per game (16th nationally). Score 81.6 PPG (57th nationally).

Defensive pressure: Not the "Havoc" press of 2011, but still disruptive. Forced UNC into mistakes down the stretch. Physical, extended pressure both full-court and half-court. UNC missed 8 free throws.
Interior vulnerability: 46.7% of opponents' points come in the paint. Only 4th-5th in A-10 rebounding. Against Illinois's twin 7-footers and Mirkovic's 17-rebound game, the boards could be a problem.

styles.title: The #1 Offense in America
bullets: Historically elite offense: KenPom #1 in adjusted offensive efficiency (125.5). Earlier this season they posted a 132.5 rating — the highest since KenPom began tracking in 2002. Five players average 10+ PPG. Score 84.4 PPG (21st nationally). This is surgical precision, not chaos.

First-round statement: Scored 105 points against Penn — an Illinois tournament record. Mirkovic had 29 and 17. Wagler had 18/7/7. Outrebounded Penn 48-25. Held Penn's 44-point Ivy League hero TJ Power to just 6 points.

Offensive rebounding machine: #4 nationally in offensive rebounding percentage (39.7%). Against Penn they had 20 offensive boards to Penn's 7. Second chances fuel the offense. This is a massive advantage against VCU's interior weakness.

International flavor: Twin 7-footers from Croatia (Tomislav and Zvonimir Ivisic), a freshman from Montenegro (Mirkovic), and Peja Stojakovic's son (Andrej). This roster is built unlike any other in the tournament.

Defensive concerns: KenPom ~105th in defensive efficiency. Allowed 84+ points in each of their losses. Athletic guards have repeatedly exposed the perimeter. Hill just scored 34 on 7-of-10 from three — and he's very athletic.

keys.title: Hill scored 34 against UNC. Illinois's perimeter defense is worse.
keys.description: Terrence Hill Jr. went 7-of-10 from three against North Carolina — a team ranked higher in defensive efficiency than Illinois. The Illini's perimeter defense has been their Achilles heel all season (KenPom ~105th). Athletic, quick guards have torched them repeatedly. Hill is exactly that kind of player. If Illinois can't find a way to contain him, VCU has the firepower to hang with the #1 offense.

keys.title: Illinois outrebounded Penn 48-25. VCU is weak inside.
keys.description: Illinois is #4 nationally in offensive rebounding with twin 7-footers and Mirkovic (who just grabbed 17 boards). VCU allows 46.7% of opponents' points in the paint and ranks 4th-5th in A-10 rebounding. The size mismatch is real. If Illinois dominates the glass like they did against Penn, the second-chance points could overwhelm VCU's depth advantage.

keys.title: VCU played overtime against UNC. Illinois rested starters in a blowout.
keys.description: VCU played 45 emotional minutes against North Carolina — including the stress of erasing a 19-point deficit and surviving overtime. Illinois played a stress-free blowout and got their starters rest in the second half. In a Saturday afternoon game, VCU's legs could be the deciding factor. Watch for scoring droughts in the second half.

keys.title: VCU's depth vs. Illinois's five-man offensive machine.
keys.description: VCU plays seven guys 20+ minutes with a bench averaging 34 PPG. Illinois has five double-figure scorers running the #1 offense in America. VCU's strategy is to wear teams down with waves; Illinois's strategy is to bury you before that matters. If VCU can keep it close through 30 minutes, their depth wins. If Illinois builds a double-digit lead early, VCU may not have another 19-point comeback in them.

keys.title: UNC's six-man rotation crumbled in OT. Does Illinois have the depth to sustain?
keys.description: UNC's downfall was a short rotation that ran out of gas. Illinois goes 9-10 deep and rested starters against Penn. But VCU's pressure is designed to create fatigue — physical, extended, switching defense that wears on legs and minds. If VCU can force turnovers and keep Illinois uncomfortable for 40 minutes, the Illini's defensive weaknesses could compound in the final stretch.

prediction.summary: This game is going to be a shootout — and the question is whether VCU's depth can overcome Illinois's sheer firepower. The Illini have the #1 offense in KenPom (125.5 adjusted) with Wagler, Mirkovic, and Stojakovic all capable of dropping 25+. But their perimeter defense (KenPom ~105th) is exactly the kind of weakness Hill exploits — he just went 7-of-10 from three against North Carolina, a better defensive team. VCU's strategy is clear: play seven deep, wear Illinois down with waves of fresh bodies, and bet that Hill stays hot. Illinois's counter: dominate the glass with the twin 7-footers and Mirkovic (17 rebounds vs. Penn), and bury VCU with second-chance points before the depth advantage matters. The emotional hangover from VCU's 19-point comeback and overtime win is the wild card nobody can predict.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Both offenses come out firing. Hill hits two early threes, and the VCU bench goes wild. Wagler answers with 8 quick points, including a pull-up three that draws comparisons to his 46-point Purdue game. The Ivisic twins and Mirkovic dominate the offensive glass — 4 offensive rebounds in the first 10 minutes. Score: Illinois 22, VCU 19. This is going to be a high-scoring affair.

phases.title: Mid-First Half to Halftime
phases.description: VCU's depth starts showing. Martelli rotates 7 players, and Illinois's perimeter defense breaks down against fresh legs. Belle comes off the bench and hits two threes (50% from deep on the season). Djokovic draws fouls on Tomislav Ivisic. But Mirkovic is unstoppable inside — 14 points at the half. Illinois leads 42-39 in a game that's living up to the billing.

phases.title: Second Half — VCU's Depth vs. Illinois's Talent
phases.description: VCU ties it at 55 on a Hill stepback three — shades of the UNC dagger. The Rams' pressure forces 3 turnovers in a 4-minute stretch. Lewis pushes pace and finds Djokovic for a layup. VCU takes a 4-point lead. But Stojakovic — Peja's son with his father's shooting DNA — hits back-to-back threes to reclaim the lead for Illinois. The teams trade blows.

phases.title: Final 5 Minutes
phases.description: The overtime fatigue finally catches VCU. Hill misses two threes he'd normally hit, and the Rams' energy dips. Wagler gets to the line and converts. Mirkovic grabs a critical offensive rebound and scores on a putback. Illinois pulls ahead by 6 with 2 minutes left, and VCU can't summon another miracle comeback. The Cinderella run ends — but Hill and the Rams leave Greenville as one of the tournament's best stories.

projectedScore.rationale: Illinois's offensive firepower and rebounding advantage ultimately overwhelm VCU after the emotional and physical toll of the overtime UNC comeback — Wagler and Mirkovic combine for 45+.

players.name: Terrence Hill Jr.
players.meta: G • 6-3 • Sophomore • A-10 Tournament MVP & Sixth Man of the Year
players.bio: The hottest player in America right now. From Portland, Oregon — Dame Lillard attended one of his high school games. Went from 3.4 PPG as a freshman to 14.4 and A-10 First Team. Scored 34 points against UNC (7-of-10 from three), including the stepback dagger with 15 seconds left in OT. Starts only 2 of 35 games but leads VCU in scoring. Named A-10 Sixth Man of the Year, Most Improved, and Tournament MVP. "When we get to that March Madness we will be ready."

players.name: Lazar Djokovic
players.meta: F • 6-11 • Junior • Serbia • A-10 First Team
players.bio: From Gornji Milanovac, Serbia. Played for Red Star Belgrade's youth academy. Transfer path: Xavier → College of Charleston → VCU. Averaged 14.9 PPG for Serbia at the FIBA U19 World Cup. Had 15 points and 5 rebounds against UNC. Skilled stretch-four who draws 4.7 fouls per game — Illinois's bigs need to stay out of foul trouble.

players.name: Nyk Lewis
players.meta: G • 6-1 • Freshman • A-10 Rookie of the Year • Washington, D.C.
players.bio: From Gonzaga College High School in D.C. Four-star recruit, one of VCU's highest-rated recruits ever. Starting point guard as a true freshman. Had 17 points and 11 rebounds in the A-10 championship game (4-of-5 from three). Scored 16 points against UNC and sealed the win with clutch free throws in OT.

players.name: Michael Belle
players.bio: From London. Played in Strasbourg's U21 program in France. A reserve who provides instant offense — career-high 20 against Saint Joseph's, scored 12 in the A-10 championship. His 50% three-point shooting and 54.8% from the field make him a lethal bench weapon. Part of VCU's 8th-best bench in America.

players.name: David Mirkovic
players.stats: 13.4 PPG • 7.8 RPG • 29 pts/17 reb vs Penn
players.meta: F • 6-8 • Freshman • Montenegro • Big Ten All-Freshman
players.bio:
From Niksic, Montenegro. Left home at 14 to play for SC Derby in Podgorica. Just had 29 points and 17 rebounds against Penn — a tournament rebound record for Illinois. Received a standing ovation when subbed out. Earlier this season: a 27-point, 21-rebound game vs. Colgate — the first 20-20 by an Illini in 53 years. Seven double-doubles. The tournament breakout star.
Read more →

players.name: Keaton Wagler
players.meta: G • 6-6 • Freshman • Unanimous Big Ten FOY • Projected Lottery Pick
players.bio:
From Lenexa, Kansas. Was 5-8, 110 pounds as a high school freshman — grew to 6-6 by senior year. Not a top-100 ESPN recruit. Scored 46 points against #4 Purdue — the most by a Big Ten freshman in 30 years. Had 18/7/7 against Penn. Unanimous Big Ten Freshman of the Year. ESPN's #6 draft prospect. Underwood: "He's that silent killer."
Read more →

players.name: Andrej Stojakovic
players.meta: G • 6-5 • Junior • Son of NBA Legend Peja Stojakovic
players.bio: Son of NBA legend Peja Stojakovic (3x All-Star, 2011 Mavs champion). From Sacramento. Played at Stanford, then Cal (17.9 PPG), then Illinois. Reached 1,000 career points. Scored 30 vs. Maryland, 26 vs. Alabama. His father's shooting DNA is real — proven shot-maker who can erupt from anywhere.

players.name: Tomislav Ivisic
players.meta: C • 7-1 • Sophomore • Croatia • Twin Brother of Zvonimir
players.bio: From Vodice, Croatia. Twin brother (younger by four minutes) of teammate Zvonimir. Played three seasons with SC Derby in the ABA League. Represented Croatia at national team level. Part of Illinois's unique twin-7-footer frontcourt — when both Ivisics are on the floor with Mirkovic, the size advantage is overwhelming.

funFacts.title: 3.4 to 34 (VCU)
funFacts.body: Terrence Hill Jr. averaged 3.4 PPG as a freshman. As a sophomore, he scored 34 against North Carolina in the NCAA Tournament — going 7-of-10 from three, including the OT dagger. Named A-10 Sixth Man of the Year, Most Improved, AND Tournament MVP. The biggest leap in college basketball.

funFacts.title: Peja's Son (Illinois)
funFacts.body: Andrej Stojakovic is the son of NBA legend Peja Stojakovic — a 3x All-Star, elite three-point shooter, and 2011 NBA champion with the Mavericks. Andrej has his father's shooting DNA: he scored 30 against Maryland and 26 against Alabama.

funFacts.title: The 2011 Cinderella Run (VCU)
funFacts.body: Under 33-year-old Shaka Smart, VCU was controversially selected to the First Four. They responded by winning five straight games to reach the Final Four — including a stunning upset of #1 Kansas. Dick Vitale had criticized the committee for putting them in. The "Havoc" defense became legendary. More →

funFacts.title: Twin 7-Footers (Illinois)
funFacts.body: Illinois has twin 7-foot brothers from Croatia: Tomislav (7-1) and Zvonimir (7-2) Ivisic. Tomislav is younger by four minutes. Zvonimir previously played at Kentucky under Calipari, then Arkansas. They reunited at Illinois. When both are on the court, the frontcourt is terrifying.

funFacts.title: Red Star to Richmond (VCU)
funFacts.body: Lazar Djokovic is from Gornji Milanovac, Serbia, and played for Red Star Belgrade's youth academy. He went from 2.8 PPG at Xavier to 13.5 at VCU. Averaged 14.9 PPG for Serbia at the FIBA U19 World Cup.

funFacts.title: Left Home at 14 (Illinois)
funFacts.body: David Mirkovic left his home in Niksic, Montenegro, at age 14 to play basketball in Podgorica. He just had 29 points and 17 rebounds in the NCAA Tournament. His 27-21 game vs. Colgate was the first 20-20 by an Illini in 53 years.

funFacts.title: Three Generations of Martellis (VCU)
funFacts.body: VCU coach Phil Martelli Jr. is the son of legendary Saint Joseph's coach Phil Sr. (currently Michigan's acting coach). After VCU won the A-10 title, three generations of Martellis cut down the nets together. Junior played alongside Jameer Nelson at St. Joe's.

funFacts.title: 105 Points (Illinois)
funFacts.body: Illinois scored 105 points against Penn — a program NCAA Tournament scoring record. They outrebounded Penn 48-25 with 20 offensive boards. Penn's TJ Power, who scored 44 in the Ivy League title game, was held to just 6.

funFacts.title: The Comeback Kings (VCU)
funFacts.body: VCU erased a 19-point second-half deficit against UNC — the largest first-round comeback in NCAA Tournament history. UNC's six-man rotation ran out of gas. VCU's depth (7 players, 20+ minutes) was the difference.

funFacts.title: Silicon Valley Was Born at Illinois
funFacts.body: Illinois alumni include Marc Andreessen (Netscape, Mosaic browser), Max Levchin (PayPal co-founder), and Jawed Karim (YouTube co-founder). The modern internet arguably starts on this campus.

funFacts.title: Erin Brockovich Went to VCU
funFacts.body: Erin Brockovich — the environmental activist played by Julia Roberts in the Oscar-winning film — attended Virginia Commonwealth University. Other VCU alumni: David Baldacci (bestselling author) and Sally Yates (former acting Attorney General).

funFacts.title: 37-2 and Heartbreak (Illinois)
funFacts.body: The 2004-05 Illinois team went 37-2 — one of the greatest seasons in college basketball history. Led by Deron Williams, they reached the championship game and lost to North Carolina 75-70. It's the closest Illinois has ever come to a national title.

history.callout: Two programs with very different tournament identities. VCU is the Cinderella factory — the 2011 Final Four run from the First Four is one of March's greatest stories. Illinois has the 37-2 heartbreak of 2005 and six straight tournament appearances under Underwood. VCU just pulled off the biggest comeback in first-round history. Illinois just set a program scoring record. Something special is brewing.

timeline.text: Under 33-year-old Shaka Smart, VCU won five straight games from the First Four to the Final Four — including a stunning upset of #1 Kansas. The "Havoc" press became legendary.
VCU's last NCAA Tournament victory before this year. A decade-long drought in March.
Down 19 to North Carolina, VCU staged the largest first-round comeback in tournament history. Won 82-78 in OT. Hill scored 34. First tournament win since 2016.

subsections.title: Illinois: The Quest for a Title

timeline.text: Lou Henson's team went 31-5 and reached the Final Four. Lost to Michigan on a last-second shot.
Deron Williams led Illinois to 37-2 and the championship game. Lost to North Carolina 75-70. The program's closest call for a national title.
Underwood's best run — Elite Eight for the first time since 2005. Lost to UConn.
Scored 105 against Penn — a program tournament record. The #1 offense in KenPom. Wagler and Mirkovic are the best freshman duo in the country.

subsections.title: The International Connection
subsections.content: This game features an extraordinary international cast. VCU has Lazar Djokovic from Serbia (Red Star Belgrade), Michael Belle from London, and a first-year coach who is the son of a legendary Italian-American basketball family. Illinois has twin 7-footers from Croatia, a freshman from Montenegro who left home at 14, and the son of Serbian-born NBA star Peja Stojakovic. College basketball's global pipeline is on full display.
